title: GDPR Resilience and Availability Monitoring for Personal-Data Services
exclusions: Planned maintenance windows documented in the change schedule; failover tests in the DR test calendar; graceful degradation modes that maintain data integrity while reducing throughput.
evidence: Resilience monitoring evidence showing service availability percentages, incident response times, recovery point objectives achieved, and any SLA breaches with root cause.

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.32(1)(b)(c) — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.32(1)(b)(c) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.26: GDPR Resilience and Availability Monitoring for Personal-Data Services.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.26' running on index=itsi_summary (service health), index=synthetic or HEC synthetic results, gdpr_ropa_register.csv, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Simulate a 30-minute outage of a service designated as processing personal data (Art.32 availability obligation); verify the resilience monitoring alerts with service name, downtime duration, and affected data categories.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Maintain test service availability at 99.95% throughout the monitoring period with no unplanned outages; verify the dashboard shows green status and no availability alerts fire.

spl:
| inputlookup gdpr_ropa_register.csv WHERE data_category!="N/A"
| fields system_name
| lookup itsi_service_map.csv system_name OUTPUT itsi_service
| join itsi_service [
    search index=itsi_summary earliest=-24h
    | stats latest(health_score) as health_score latest(severity) as severity by itsi_service
]
| eval breach_slo=if(health_score < 70 OR match(lower(severity),"(?i)critical|high"), 1, 0)
| where breach_slo=1
| table system_name, itsi_service, health_score, severity
description: Integrity, confidentiality, and availability of processing systems are core Art. 32 objectives. This use case monitors uptime, error rates, and recovery objectives for applications and databases documented in the ROPA as processing personal data — evidencing resilience measures such as clustering, failover drills, and synthetic checks.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes itsi_summary, synthetic. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Map ROPA `system_name` to ITSI services in `itsi_service_map.csv`; (2) define KPI thresholds aligned to internal RPO/RTO for personal-data services; (3) alert when health scores breach SLO; (4) attach failover drill references from `gdpr_security_test_register.csv`; (5) report monthly resilience posture to the DPO. Indexes required: `index=itsi_summary`, `index=synthetic`.
visualization: Single value (services breaching SLO), Table (affected personal-data services), Line chart (health score trend), Heatmap (severity by hour).
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Planned maintenance windows** — Scheduled downtime for database maintenance, patching, or upgrades generates availability alerts that are pre-approved in the change schedule.

2. **Failover testing** — Planned failover tests between primary and secondary data centres create brief availability gaps that are part of the resilience validation programme.

3. **Auto-scaling events** — Cloud services scaling down during low-demand periods may temporarily reduce redundancy levels while maintaining the required minimum; this is by design.

4. **Monitoring system false negatives** — Health check failures due to monitoring infrastructure issues (network partition to the monitor, not the service) may report downtime when the service is operational.

5. **Graceful degradation modes** — Services operating in degraded mode (read-only, cached responses) during partial outages still protect personal data integrity while showing reduced availability metrics.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`gdpr_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by process owner, approval reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries quarterly.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `itsi_summary` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `itsi_service_map.csv`, `gdpr_ropa_register.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('itsi_service_map.csv','gdpr_ropa_register.csv')`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.32(1)(b)(c)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.32(1)(b)(c) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.26: GDPR Resilience and Availability Monitoring for Personal-Data Services." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `itsi_summary` under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Map ROPA `system_name` to ITSI services in `itsi_service_map.csv`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** define KPI thresholds aligned to internal RPO/RTO for personal-data services. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** alert when health scores breach SLO. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** attach failover drill references from `gdpr_security_test_register.csv`. The register referenced here is primary evidence — treat changes to it with the same discipline as code changes.

• **Hint 5:** report monthly resilience posture to the DPO. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "GDPR Resilience and Availability Monitoring for Personal-Data Services," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the GDPR control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search correlates the outer dataset (index=`itsi_summary`, sourcetype=`the primary`) with a subsearch on `itsi_service` across the last 24 hours. `type=inner` means the search only returns rows where `itsi_service` exists in both datasets. This is the correct choice when the detection requires presence in both sources — a left-join would include orphaned rows that cannot be evaluated against the compliance rule.

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.1.26: GDPR Resilience and Availability Monitoring for Personal-Dat]
cron_schedule = 0 1 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.1.26,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=itsi_summary sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `breach_slo`, `health_score`, `itsi_service`, `severity`, `system_name`. Run:

```spl
index=itsi_summary sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table breach_slo, health_score, itsi_service, severity, system_name
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `itsi_service_map.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup itsi_service_map.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `health_score`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(health_score) as p50 perc90(health_score) as p90 perc99(health_score) as p99 max(health_score) as hi
```

The current threshold `70` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.1.26: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Single-value KPI (`services breaching SLO`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'GDPR current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.

**Panel 2 — Drill-through table (`affected personal-data services`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 3 — Line Chart (`health score trend`):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 4 — Heatmap (`severity by hour`):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We integrity, confidentiality, and availability of processing systems are core Art so we can show we meet our privacy duties when people ask for proof.
